claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin assessing your eligibility for Child Benefit.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is your child?**

Please let me know if they are:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me each of their ages and I'll assess them all.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 13 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Alex, at 13 years old, falls into the **under 16** category, which is great news as children under 16 automatically qualify based on age.

Let me continue with the next step of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

At 13 years old, I imagine Alex isn't in full-time employment, but I need to confirm this. Most children of this age either don't work at all or may have a small part-time job or paper round.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all. He's just focused on school at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's exactly what I expected for a 13-year-old focused on school. Let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, I need to confirm that Alex is not personally claiming benefits such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

This would be quite unusual for a 13-year-old, so I expect the answer is that he's not receiving any such benefits. Can you confirm?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a regular 13-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thanks for confirming. Let me move on to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?**

At 13 years old and focused on school, I expect he's not in any apprenticeship programme. But just to confirm - is Alex:

- **Not in any apprenticeship** (most likely for a 13-year-old)
- In any kind of apprenticeship programme

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship. He's too young for that - he's still in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right - he's too young for that! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common)
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad (e.g., working for the UK government overseas)

Which of these applies to you? For most people, it's simply that they normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. We've been here all along.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's straightforward - living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirements. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You're considered responsible for a child if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** (most common)
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** - paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards his care (this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** for Alex under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's the most straightforward way to establish responsibility. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This could be:
- **No one else is claiming** - you would be the only claimant
- **Someone else is claiming but has agreed to transfer the claim to you** - for example, if Alex recently moved in with you
- **There's a dispute** - someone else is also trying to claim and you haven't agreed who should receive it

Which situation applies?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things simple. Let me proceed to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Where does Alex currently live?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply. Is Alex:

- **Living at home normally** (the usual situation)
- **In hospital** for an extended period
- **In local authority care**
- **Being fostered**

Which of these describes Alex's current situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living at home normally with me. Nothing unusual - just the regular family situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, a straightforward family situation. Let me continue with the final checks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost done! Just one final question.

**Is it true that the local council is NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, the council isn't contributing financially to Alex's care or housing costs. For most families, the answer is yes - the council is not involved in paying for the child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. We're just a regular family - I provide for him myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful! That completes the assessment. Let me navigate to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! Based on all the information you've provided, I can now give you the outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Good News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

Based on our assessment, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

### Summary for Alex:
✅ Under 16 years old (13)  
✅ Not working full-time  
✅ Not receiving benefits in his own right  
✅ Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship  
✅ You live in the UK  
✅ Alex lives with you  
✅ No other claimant  
✅ Normal living situation  
✅ Council not paying for his care  

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important Note:
⚠️ If you or your partner earns over **£60,000** per year, you may be affected by the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**. You can still claim, but some or all of it may need to be repaid through your tax return.

### Useful Link:
🔗 [How to Claim Child Benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old (13), which automatically qualifies him based on age; (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right such as ESA or Universal Credit; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living at home normally; and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for their son Alex. This is a straightforward case of a UK-resident parent with a 13-year-old child living at home in a normal family situation with no complicating factors.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old (13), which automatically qualifies him based on age; (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right such as ESA or Universal Credit; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living at home normally; and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for their son Alex. This is a straightforward case of a UK-resident parent with a 13-year-old child living at home in a normal family situation with no complicating factors.
